structure.audience: Engineers building platforms that execute untrusted or AI-agent-generated code, who must choose isolation layers deliberately and understand where each boundary actually is.
structure.rationale: The book climbs the isolation stack in the order a defender assembles it: first the threat model (what you are defending against and what your trusted computing base is), then each Linux primitive one at a time (namespaces, cgroups, capabilities, seccomp, chroot/pivot_root), then how they compose into a container, then a history of real escapes that show where the shared-kernel boundary fails, then the stronger boundaries (microVMs and WebAssembly/capability models), and finally a decision guide for layering them. Each primitive is introduced with the mechanism it addresses and the threat it does — and does not — stop.

claims.text: Sandboxing is defined relative to a threat model: what the attacker controls, what they want, and which component boundary must hold.
For a container, the trusted computing base includes the shared host kernel, so any kernel local-privilege-escalation bug is a potential escape.
Least privilege and defense in depth are the governing principles: assume the workload is hostile and stack independent boundaries so no single bug is fatal.

pitfalls: Treating 'it runs in a container' as a security guarantee without asking which boundary is doing the work and what its TCB is.

claims.text: There are 8 namespace types — mount, PID, network, IPC, UTS, user, cgroup, and time — each partitioning one global kernel resource so processes inside see an isolated instance.
The mount namespace (CLONE_NEWNS) was the first, added in Linux 2.4.19, which is why the generic flag is named 'NS'.
User namespace functionality was completed in Linux 3.8, the cgroup namespace was added in Linux 4.6, and the time namespace in Linux 5.6.

Three syscalls drive the API: clone(2) starts a child in new namespaces via CLONE_NEW* flags, unshare(2) moves the calling process into new namespaces, and setns(2) joins an existing namespace via an fd such as /proc/[pid]/ns/*.
The user namespace lets a process be root inside it with a full capability set while remaining unprivileged on the host, which is the basis of rootless containers.

snippets.note: create a new set of namespaces and run a shell inside them

pitfalls: A namespace isolates a view, not privilege — a process can still be over-powerful inside its own namespaces (see capabilities).

claims.text: cgroups v2 uses a single unified hierarchy at /sys/fs/cgroup/; every process belongs to exactly one cgroup and all enabled controllers apply together.
Core v2 controllers are cpu, memory, io, and pids (plus hugetlb, cpuset, rdma, misc).
Limits are set by writing interface files: cpu.max (quota/period hard cap), memory.max (hard memory limit), io.max (per-device caps), and pids.max (process-count cap).
Controllers are enabled top-down via cgroup.subtree_control, and a child can only use a controller its parent has already enabled.

snippets.code:
echo '50000 100000' > /sys/fs/cgroup/jail/cpu.max   # 50% of one CPU
echo 268435456    > /sys/fs/cgroup/jail/memory.max # 256 MiB
echo 64           > /sys/fs/cgroup/jail/pids.max   # fork-bomb ceiling

pitfalls: cgroups bound resource consumption, not privilege — they stop a fork bomb or memory hog, not an escape. pids.max is the specific defense against fork bombs.

claims.text: Capabilities split the monolithic power of root (UID 0) into distinct units that can be independently granted or dropped.
The highest defined capability is CAP_CHECKPOINT_RESTORE = 40 (added in Linux 5.9), so CAP_LAST_CAP = 40 and there are 41 capabilities total (0–40).

Each thread has five capability sets: permitted, effective, inheritable, the bounding set (a ceiling on what can ever be gained), and the ambient set (inheritable across execve for unprivileged programs).

CAP_SYS_ADMIN is a catch-all covering mount and namespace operations and much more — effectively 'the new root' and the single most abused capability; CAP_SYS_PTRACE, CAP_NET_ADMIN, CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE, and CAP_SYS_CHROOT are also dangerous.

snippets.note: drop dangerous capabilities from the bounding set before running a payload

pitfalls: Granting CAP_SYS_ADMIN to an untrusted workload effectively hands it root; treat it as never-grant.
Dropping a capability from the effective set but leaving it in the bounding/inheritable set can let it be regained.

claims.text: seccomp has two modes: SECCOMP_MODE_STRICT allows only read, write, _exit, and sigreturn (any other syscall triggers SIGKILL); SECCOMP_MODE_FILTER runs a classic-BPF program over struct seccomp_data (syscall number plus up to 6 arguments).

Filter return actions, in priority order, are KILL_PROCESS/KILL_THREAD, TRAP (raises SIGSYS), ERRNO (fail with a chosen errno), TRACE, USER_NOTIF, LOG, and ALLOW; libseccomp exposes SCMP_ACT_KILL, SCMP_ACT_TRAP, SCMP_ACT_ERRNO, SCMP_ACT_NOTIFY, and SCMP_ACT_ALLOW.

seccomp user notification (SECCOMP_RET_USER_NOTIF, since Linux 5.0) blocks the target in-kernel and notifies a supervisor process, which can emulate privileged syscalls — heavily used by rootless containers.
Docker's default seccomp profile is an allowlist that blocks roughly 44 of 300+ syscalls, including keyctl, mount/umount, reboot, kexec_load, and bpf.

snippets.note: seccomp filters inspect the syscall number in seccomp_data.nr

pitfalls: seccomp filters syscalls by number/args but cannot dereference pointer arguments (TOCTOU), so it cannot inspect a path string — combine with other layers.
A filter must be installed with NO_NEW_PRIVS or CAP_SYS_ADMIN, and cannot be relaxed once set.

claims.text: chroot() is not a security boundary: it changes the apparent root but does not change the current working directory and does not close file descriptors opened before the call.

The classic chroot escape opens a directory fd, calls chroot() into a subdir, then fchdir()s back to the saved fd and walks ../ out to the real root; self-jailing programs must also drop CAP_SYS_CHROOT and chdir into the new root.
Containers instead use pivot_root(2) inside a private mount namespace to swap the root mount for the container rootfs and unmount the old root, leaving no path back to the host tree.

snippets.note: the fchdir chroot escape — why chroot alone contains nothing

pitfalls: pivot_root restricts only the filesystem view; network, IPC, and syscalls still need their own namespaces and seccomp.

claims.text: A container is not a kernel object; it is an assembly of primitives — namespaces + cgroups + seccomp + capabilities + pivot_root/mount-ns + optionally user namespaces.

runc implements the OCI Runtime Specification — the vendor-neutral definition of namespaces, cgroups, capabilities, seccomp profile, mounts, hooks, and the process to exec — and is the low-level runtime under Docker, containerd, and CRI-O.

The runtime lifecycle is roughly: parse the spec, clone/unshare the configured namespaces, set up cgroups, mount the rootfs, pivot_root, apply capabilities and the seccomp filter, then execve the target.
Rootless containers use a user namespace so an unprivileged user is root inside, and can use seccomp user-notification (Linux 5.0+) to let a userspace supervisor handle privileged syscalls.

pitfalls: Because a container shares the host kernel, its isolation is only as strong as the weakest primitive plus the kernel's own robustness.

claims.text: CVE-2019-5736: a malicious container overwrites the host runc binary via /proc/self/exe — it points an entry binary at #!/proc/self/exe so that when runc re-execs it, the attacker writes through /proc/self/exe to clobber host runc and gain root on the host.

CVE-2022-0492: cgroup_release_agent_write() in cgroups v1 failed to check for CAP_SYS_ADMIN, letting a container that mounts cgroupfs and sets notify_on_release + release_agent make the host kernel execute an attacker binary as root; fixed around 5.17-rc3 and blocked by AppArmor/SELinux.

CVE-2016-5195 'Dirty COW': a race condition in copy-on-write handling of private read-only mappings let an unprivileged local user write to read-only memory and escalate to root; present since 2.6.22, fixed in 4.8.3/4.7.9/4.4.26.

CVE-2024-21626 'Leaky Vessels': an internal file-descriptor leak in runc lets a container's working directory resolve into the host filesystem namespace, enabling escape; affects runc <= 1.1.11, fixed in runc 1.1.12.

CVE-2024-0132: a TOCTOU race in the NVIDIA Container Toolkit's file mounting lets a crafted image mount host directories into itself and escape (CVSS 9.0); affects Container Toolkit <= 1.16.1, fixed in 1.16.2.

pitfalls: Most escapes exploit a shared-kernel or host-binary trust assumption, not a namespace bug — which is exactly why a separate kernel boundary (microVM) raises the bar.

claims.text: Firecracker is a KVM-based VMM with a deliberately minimal device model (about six emulated devices), documented at under 125 ms startup to application code and under 5 MiB memory overhead per microVM, and backs AWS Lambda and AWS Fargate.
The small device surface and Rust implementation shrink the host-facing attack surface relative to a full QEMU.
gVisor runs a userspace application kernel called the Sentry that intercepts a guest's syscalls and re-implements the Linux API in userspace, so guest syscalls do not hit the host kernel directly.
gVisor's syscall-interception platforms are ptrace, KVM, and systrap; a separate Gofer process brokers filesystem access and the Sentry runs with a tightly restricted set of host syscalls.

A container shares the single host kernel (any kernel LPE is a potential escape), whereas a microVM interposes a separate guest kernel plus a hardware virtualization boundary, so an attacker must defeat the guest kernel and the VMM.

pitfalls: Hardware virtualization is not infinite: hypervisor and CPU side-channel bugs still exist, and gVisor trades some compatibility (unimplemented syscalls) for its smaller host surface.

claims.text: A WebAssembly module runs in a linear-memory sandbox: its memory is a single bounds-checked byte array with no ambient access to host memory, pointers, or syscalls — it can only call functions the host explicitly imports.
WASI is capability-based with no ambient authority: a module cannot open arbitrary paths or sockets, and the host must explicitly grant capabilities such as preopened directories at instantiation.

WASI Preview 1 exposed a flat set of preopened directory fds, while Preview 2 rebuilds WASI on the Component Model where every external resource is a typed, unforgeable handle, enabling finer-grained grants.
This realizes the object-capability model: authority is conveyed only by possessing an unforgeable reference, so a component holds no more authority than it was explicitly handed.

pitfalls: WASM sandboxes the module's memory and imports, but a bug in the host runtime or a too-generous capability grant (e.g. preopening '/') reopens the hole.

claims.text: The layers compose: namespaces hide resources, cgroups ration them, capabilities and seccomp reduce privilege and syscall surface, pivot_root fixes the filesystem, and a microVM or WASM boundary contains a kernel or runtime break.

Choice of boundary follows the threat model: trusted-ish first-party code can run in a hardened container, while genuinely untrusted or AI-agent-generated code that needs the shared kernel is better placed behind a microVM or a WASM/capability sandbox.
No single layer is sufficient — history shows each has been bypassed — so least privilege and independent, redundant boundaries are the durable strategy.

pitfalls: Adding layers without understanding each boundary's TCB gives false confidence; know which layer stops which threat.

volatile: Linux kernel version numbers and per-feature gate versions
